title: Local Current Algebra for the HK Universality Class
abstract: We show that a Hamiltonian in terms of the local real-space currents obeying an $\mathfrak{su}_1(2)$ affine Lie algebra eliminates the non-locality in the Hatsugai-Kohmoto model for a doped Mott insulator. We establish this local correspondence through the Bjorken-Johnson-Low prescription for anomalous commutators. With this result, we show that the charge susceptibility computed from the current Hamiltonian is identical to that with the elemental Fermionic fields. Consequently, the HK model is local in real space, though not in terms of the Fermionic fields, thereby eliminating the key criticism of this model and reinforcing the utility of current algebras for strong interactions.
summary: This paper resolves a major criticism of the Hatsugai-Kohmoto model by showing that its apparent non-locality in real space can be eliminated using a local current algebra. By establishing a local correspondence, the authors reinforce the utility of current algebras for describing strong interactions in Mott insulators.
main_problem: The paper addresses the non-locality in real space of the Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) model, a known criticism of this model for doped Mott insulators.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a Hamiltonian expressed via local real-space currents obeying an su(2) affine Lie algebra eliminates the non-locality, and that the charge susceptibility computed from this current Hamiltonian is identical to the original fermionic formulation.
method: The study uses the Bjorken-Johnson-Low (BJL) prescription to derive equal-time commutation relations from microscopic correlation functions and employs a current algebra formulation (Sugawara-like construction) in the Dirac limit.
model_or_system: The system is a 1D doped Mott insulator described by the Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) model, which is a solvable alternative to the Hubbard model that captures Mott physics via the breaking of Z2 symmetry.
key_observables: Charge susceptibility (direct and mixing components), density operators, and current operators.
important_parameters: Interaction strength U, Fermi momentum kF, Fermi velocity vF, and the Dirac limit (kF, m -> infinity).
main_assumptions: The dispersion is assumed to be quadratic, and the results are presented in the low-energy, long-wavelength (q -> 0) limit.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the non-locality problem in the HK model, then introduces the current algebra formulation. It uses the BJL prescription to derive commutators, proves the necessity of multi-component currents, and concludes by verifying that the resulting susceptibility matches the original model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
